sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the current state clearly:
- Node 8: color=b, pixels=16, boundary=[[20, 18], [20, 21], [23, 21], [23, 18]] - blue square at (20, 18)-(23, 21)
- Node 11: color=R, pixels=24, boundary=[[24, 18], [24, 21], [29, 21], [29, 18]] - red block at (24, 18)-(29, 21)
- Node 14: color=r, pixels=16, boundary=[[30, 18], [30, 21], [33, 21], [33, 18]] - dark red block at (30, 18)-(33, 21)
- Checkerboard at (34, 8)-(37, 11) - this is the small blue/charcoal pixels
- Green square at (38, 12)-(39, 13)
So after the blue click:
- Blue square moved from (34, 8)-(37, 11) to (20, 18)-(23, 21)
- Checkerboard moved from (20, 18)-(23, 21) to (34, 8)-(37, 11)
- Red block stayed at (24, 18)-(29, 21)
- Dark red block stayed at (30, 18)-(33, 21)
So the blue click swaps the blue square with the checkerboard. The blue square moved to the top of the middle column, and the checkerboard moved to the bottom-left.
Now the configuration is:
- Middle column (top to bottom): blue square (20-23), red block (24-29), dark red block (30-33)
- Left column: gray block (30-33), checkerboard (34-37)
- Green square at (38, 12)
This looks like a stacked configuration. The blue square, red block, and dark red block are stacked vertically in the middle column. Maybe this is part of the solution?
Let me think about what the goal might be. The right panel has two cross-shaped targets:
- Red cross: 47 pixels
- Blue cross: 47 pixels
The left panel has objects that can be rearranged. Maybe the goal is to arrange the objects to form cross shapes similar to the targets?
Or maybe the goal is to match the colors: the red cross target corresponds to the red/dark red blocks, and the blue cross target corresponds to the blue square/checkerboard.
Let me try clicking the red target again to see what happens with the current configuration.
